narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 54% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your profile, including your first-year decile ranking of 1 out of 10—far below the typical student's midpoint around the 6th decile—along with a similarly low third-year decile of 1 compared to the average near 5.5, a low family income bracket, an LSAT score of 19 that's well under the dataset average of about 37, and an undergraduate GPA of 3.0 slightly below the norm of 3.21. You're also an Asian female planning full-time studies, which aligns with common patterns but didn't outweigh the concerns. The model's decision was most heavily swayed by your third-year decile, as it strongly signaled a higher risk of bar failure compared to peers. Right behind that was your first-year decile, which reinforced worries about consistent academic performance from early on. Your LSAT score came next in influence, pulling the prediction toward failure because it's so much lower than what successful students typically show. Low family income followed closely, as it often correlates with added barriers to bar passage in our data. Your racial background as Asian had a notable but slightly lesser push in the same direction, while your undergraduate GPA added a bit more concern, though not as dominantly. Being female nudged things slightly toward a better outcome, and full-time status had minimal impact. Overall, your early and later school rankings, combined with test scores, stood out as the biggest drags compared to admitted students who usually rank higher and score stronger on the LSAT. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can control: retake the LSAT with targeted prep to aim closer to average scores, build stronger study habits proven to lift decile rankings, and seek scholarships or support programs for low-income students to mimic profiles of bar passers. With dedication to these steps, you could shift your predicted odds significantly for the next application cycle. Keep pushing—you have the drive to turn this around.
